brief: GAP/RATIONALE: Patients after AF catheter ablation often need redo procedures (PV re-isolation and/or extra-PV ablation); pulsed field ablation (PFA) is tissue-selective, potentially less collateral damage; feasibility/efficacy of redo ablation with pentaspline PFA system not established. DESIGN/SETTING: Prospective, multicenter experience; 3 international centers. POPULATION/N: 117 patients undergoing redo ablation with pentaspline PFA system. Stratified by presenting rhythm into 3 workflow groups - Group 1 (sinus rhythm) n=64; Group 2 (atrial flutter/atrial tachycardia) n=18; Group 3 (AF) n=35. INTERVENTION/COMPARISON: Redo ablation using pentaspline PFA system; propensity score matching used to compare PFA- vs RF-based redo ablations. RESULTS: PV re-isolation required in 71.9% of Group 1 and 72.2% of Group 2. PFA terminated 100% of non-CTI-dependent (non-cavotricuspid isthmus dependent) flutter cases and 45.7% of AF cases. One major complication: 0.9% (frontal cerebral hematoma). Freedom from atrial tachyarrhythmias at 12 months = 78.3% (95% CI 69.6-84.8%); no statistically significant difference among groups (Group 1: 85.7%; Group 2: 77%; Group 3: 65.5%; p=0.053). PFA gave similar arrhythmia freedom vs RF but with significantly shorter procedural and dwelling times. TAKEAWAY: Pentaspline PFA for repeat/redo ablation was feasible, safe, and effective at 1-year follow-up; no clinical efficacy difference vs RF, but redo PFA cases significantly shorter in duration.
